text: 1. High-density coherent NiAl clusters $( \sim 4 . 8 8 \times 1 0 ^ { 2 4 } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 3 } )$ with high nucleation rates and uniform dispersion were successfully introduced. A small fraction of NiAl clusters exhibits a specific interaction with Si, which increases the availability of heterogeneous nucleation sites.
2. NiAl clusters optimized dislocation distributions, thereby enhancing strain hardening, plastic deformation capacity, and delaying crack initiation.

3. Toughening mechanisms including large dimple growth, extensive plastic deformation, secondary crack formation via voids coalescence, and frequent crack deflection significantly enhanced impact energy absorption.

4. The NiAl-cluster-strengthened steel exhibited a YS of $1 2 7 2 \pm 5 2$ MPa, a UTS of $1 8 7 6 \pm 2 5 \mathrm { M P a } ,$ , a TE of 13.7 ± 0.4 %, a KU₂ of 80 ± 3 J, and a K of $1 0 8 \mathrm { \ M P a } { \cdot } \mathrm { m } ^ { 1 / 2 } ;$ , demonstrating excellent strengthtoughness balance. Overall, the uniform precipitation of NiAl clusters offers an effective pathway for enhancing the strength-toughness synergy of low alloy martensitic steels, providing insights for the design of next-generation ultrahigh strength materials. ![](images/177ded7e8f4564796096a3e9fc0b8f6f6466fa1f96b4bcd6e3914e0aaf7c39ad.jpg) <details> <summary>text_image</summary> 0Al 1.3Al High stress NiAl	ε-carbide	dislocation	secondary crack	void </details> Fig. 11. Schematic illustration of the crack propagation behavior during impact fracture. # CRediT authorship contribution statement Xiaohan Duan: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xin Cao: Formal analysis. Yangxin Wang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology. Hongshan Zhao: Formal analysis. Chundong Hu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology.
